original_abstract:
This article provides a hermeneutical exploration of the various historical contexts that converge in Question and Answer 45 of the Heidelberg Catechism, specifically regarding the central theme of the resurrection of Jesus Christ. The study seeks more than a mere surface-level interpretation of the Catechism; it aims to uncover the underlying hermeneutical frameworks that inform the text and to engage with the alternative theological assumptions that contemporary churchgoers often find unfamiliar or challenging. By demonstrating how different interpretive processes can lead to vastly different conclusions—sometimes worlds apart—this research highlights the necessity of grounding faith in the objective historical reality of the resurrection as understood by the Reformers and the Early Church.

Hierdie artikel is ’n poging om vanuit ’n hermeneutiese perspektief die verskillende historiese kontekste te verken wat mekaar beïnvloed rondom die sentrale punt van Jesus Christus se opstanding in vraag en antwoord 45 van die Heidelbergse Kategismus. Die doel is nie net om die oorspronklike betekenis van die Kategismus te ontsluit nie, maar ook om die onderliggende hermeneutiese dinkprosesse te verduidelik. Daarbenewens bring die artikel alternatiewe aannames na vore wat vir die deursnee kerkganger vreemd mag voorkom weens ’n verskil in benadering. Verskillende hermeneutiese prosesse lei onvermydelik tot uiteenlopende uitkomste van verstaan – uitkomste wat dikwels hemelsbreed van mekaar verskil.
humanized_abstract:
This article focuses on a hermeneutical analysis of the numerous historical circumstances that are condensed in Heidelberg Catechism Question and Answer 45, in an attempt to get behind the surface-level meaning of the statement that Jesus Christ is risen from the dead. It endeavours to provide an insight into the hermeneutics that underpin the Catechism, and to grapple with the alternate theological understandings that are no longer familiar to modern day church members. It makes clear how different hermeneutics can lead to vastly different interpretations - in some cases leading to conclusions that are totally disparate. The research aims to demonstrate that, whatever interpretations may be reached, it is vital to centre faith and its practice on the historical resurrection of Jesus as understood by the Reformers and the Early Church.

This article endeavours to hermeneutically explore the various historical contexts which mutually impacted each other surrounding the central theme of Jesus Christ’s resurrection as dealt with in question and answer 45 of the Heidelberg Catechism. Aim: - To shed light on the original meaning of the Catechism, and to provide greater insight into the hermeneutic process behind it. - To introduce alternative assumptions that might bewilder the average congregation member due to a vastly different approach. Consequence of different hermeneutic processes inevitably results in diverse understandings of meaning, which may differ from each other in fact up to the point of being poles apart.
